stem: A straight mountain road rises at a 37-degree angle from the horizontal. A cyclist rides 100 feet along the road. Given that sin 37° ≈ 3/5, cos 37° ≈ 4/5, and tan 37° ≈ 3/4, what horizontal distance has the cyclist covered, in feet?
explanation: The cyclist travels 100 feet along the road (the hypotenuse), and the horizontal distance is the side adjacent to the 37° angle. Using cosine: cos 37° = adjacent/hypotenuse = horizontal/100. Therefore, horizontal = 100 × cos 37° = 100 × (4/5) = 80 feet.

distractorRationale.A: Correct: cos 37° = adjacent/hypotenuse, so horizontal distance = 100 × (4/5) = 80.
distractorRationale.B: Used sin instead of cos, finding the vertical rise instead of the horizontal distance: 100 × sin 37° = 100 × (3/5) = 60.
distractorRationale.C: Used tan instead of cos, treating the hypotenuse as the opposite side: 100 × tan 37° = 100 × (3/4) = 75.
distractorRationale.D: Set up the cosine ratio inverted, dividing by cos instead of multiplying: horizontal = 100 ÷ (4/5) = 100 × (5/4) = 125.

imageDescription: A right triangle showing a mountain road as the hypotenuse labeled 100 ft. The angle between the road and the horizontal ground is labeled 37°. The horizontal leg is labeled with a question mark representing the horizontal distance covered. The vertical leg represents the elevation gain.
